messages.content:
## Ground rules:
- Follow the user's instructions. 
- DO NOT wrap answers in markdown code blocks, which means no triple backticks like ``` nor single backtick like ` .
- Do not explain answers, just give me code. 
- If changing existing code, preserve unrelated code and comments. 
- Never add comments to the end of a line. 
- Never add stupid comments.
- Be considerate with indentation. 
- Prefer readable code over of comments. 
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Recent yanks (copy to clipboard):
```fish/load_last_interactive_only/git-specific.fish
abbr gls "git log --stat $_unpushed_commits_without_last_pushed"
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Fish Shell Code Preferences

- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- When a condition is ambiguous, assign it to a well‑named variable before the `if`.
- Prefer long options, i.e. `--quiet` over `-q`, `--regex` over `-r`
- Fish shell does not do word splitting like bash, no need to wrap everything in quotes!

## Fish syntax examples

```fish
# exploit fish-isms!

# use `not`, avoid `!`
if not test -f spoon
    echo There is no spoon
    exit 1
end

# if + set
if set -l message (some_command)
    echo "ok: $message"
else
    echo "fail: $message"
end

# prefer named arguments
function show_variable --argument-names name value
    echo "$name = $value"
end

# fish doesn't have heredocs, use begin/end
begin
    echo $header
    echo $table
end > out.html
# or multi-line strings
echo "
foo
bar
" > test

# every variable is an array
set names wes john bob jane
for name in $names
    echo $name
end

for i in *.tmp
    if grep DONE $i
        break
    end
    if grep SKIP $i
        continue
    end
    ...
end

# combine conditions
while test -f foo.txt; or test -f bar.txt
    ...
end

# conditional execution
make; and make install; or make clean
```

### builtin fish commands

Now that we have more than 2KB of system RAM, we can fix most of what sucks about bash...

```fish
# *** string command

# match: test, filter, extract
string match --quiet '*.lua' $file
set lua_files (string match '*.lua' $files)
set version (string match --regex 'v([0-9.]+)' $tag)
string match --index --regex 'ERROR' $line

# replace: transform text
set fixed (string replace foo bar $text)
set cleaned (string replace --regex '\s+' ' ' $line)
set base (string replace --regex '\.[^.]+$' '' $file)
set normalized (string replace --filter --regex '^./' '' $paths)

# split: tokenize
set parts (string split ':' $PATH)
set lines (string split '\n' $text)
set ext (string split --right --max 1 . $file)[2]

# trim: clean edges
set clean (string trim $line)
set unquoted (string trim --chars '"' $s)

# join: build strings
string join ' ' $argv
string join --no-empty ':' $PATH

# length and slicing
string length $name
string sub --start 2 --length 4 $word
string sub --start -5 $word

# case conversion
set key (string lower $key)
set name (string upper $name)

# shell-safe encoding
set escaped (string escape $path)
string unescape $escaped

# normalize piped output
set text (some_command | string collect)


# path command
path basename foo/bar.txt # bar.txt
path basename --no-extension foo/bar.txt # bar
path dirname foo/bar.txt # foo
path extension ./foo.mp4 # .mp4
path change-extension mov ./foo.mp4 # ./foo.mov

path normalize path/to//hello.txt # path/to/hello.txt
path normalize path/to/../hello.txt # path/hello.txt
# resolve = normalize + absolute path and follow symlinks
path resolve foo  # ~/foo

path sort 10-foo 2-bar
# 2-bar
# 10-foo


# math command
math [(-s | --scale) N] [(-b | --base) BASE] [(-m | --scale-mode) MODE] EXPRESSION ...
math 10 / 6
math $status - 128
math "5 * 2"
math "sin(pi)"
math max 5,2,3,1


# count STRING1 STRING2 ...
# COMMAND | count
# count [...] < FILE
count $PATH
count *.txt
git ls-files --others --exclude-standard | count
printf '%s\n' foo bar | count baz # Returns 3 (2 lines from stdin plus 1 argument)
count < /etc/hosts # number of entries in hosts file


# contains [OPTIONS] KEY [VALUES ...]
if contains -- -q $argv
    echo '$argv contains a -q option'
end

# scripts
status filename # current running script
status basename
status dirname
status function # current function name
status line-number
status stack-trace

```

# Semantic Grep matches: 5

This is automatic context based on my request. These may or may not be relevant.
## ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles/fish/load_last_interactive_only/misc-specific.fish:1085-1108
function toggle-git_commit_command
    # toggle wrapping current command line in a git commit
    # to commit changes w.r.t. running a command
    # for example:
    #   uv add ipykernel
    #   => git commit -m 'uv add ipykernel'
    set -f cmd (commandline)
    if test -z "$cmd"
        # if empty cmd, then use last command
        # pull back last non git command (so I can stage files and then commit)
        set cmd (history | rg --no-config --no-line-number -v '^git\s' | head -n 1)
    end

    if string match --quiet --regex "^git\scommit\s-m\s" -- $cmd
        # unwrap
        # use regex capture group to extract the message part only (strip quotes too)
        set cmd (string replace --regex -- "^git\scommit\s-m\s'(.*)'" "\1" -- $cmd)
        commandline -r $cmd
    else
        # wrap
        commandline -r "git commit -m '$cmd'"
        return
    end
end

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles/fish/load_last_interactive_only/gitignore-wrappers.fish:8-10

    zsh -ic "gitignores_for $argv"
end

## *** fish related

    zsh -ic "commit_gitignores_for $argv"
end

bind_both_modes_default_and_insert ctrl-q toggle-grc # terrible key choice (ctrl+q) but it isn't used currently so yeah

add optional trailing f which isnerts --pretty=full like glsf right above

Here is the code I selected:
```fish/load_last_interactive_only/git-specific.fish:104-107
abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX
function glsX
    string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $argv
end
```

This is not necessarily a complete selection of nearby code, this is just the part I want help with. Preserve indentation!

response_message.content:
abbr --regex 'gls\d+f?' --function glsX _glsX
function glsX
    set -l cmd_input $argv
    set -l has_pretty false
    if string match --quiet --regex 'f$' $cmd_input
        set has_pretty true
        set cmd_input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $cmd_input)
    end

    set -l cmd (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $cmd_input)
    if $has_pretty
        set cmd "$cmd --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $cmd
end
response_message.reasoning_content:
The user wants to add optional trailing f which inserts `--pretty=full` like glsf right above. The code snippet defines an abbreviation: `abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX` and a function `glsX` which does: `string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $argv`. The user wants to add optional trailing `f` which inserts `--pretty=full` like glsf right above.

Thus there is likely a function glsf defined elsewhere that uses `--pretty=full`. They want to modify `glsX` (or perhaps define a new function glsfX?). The snippet is for generic `gls` abbreviation that matches gls followed by digits. They want optional trailing f to add the flag. So the abbreviation pattern could be something like `gls\d+f?` where `f` optional. Then the function should detect presence of trailing f and include `--pretty=full` in the command.

Specifically, the abbreviation is defined as `abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX`. The function glsX currently takes `$argv` (the abbreviation typed) and replaces leading `gls` with `git log --stat -`. So e.g. `gls10` becomes `git log --stat -10`. They want optional trailing f: e.g. `gls10f` should become `git log --stat -10 --pretty=full`. So we need to modify regex to include optional trailing f: `'gls\d+f?'` (or maybe `'gls\d+(?:f)?'` but fish regex doesn't support non-capturing groups; we can just use `gls[0-9]+f?`). Also the replacement should add `--pretty=full` if f present. We could parse `$argv` to detect trailing f, maybe using string replace, substring, or regex capture groups.

Simplest: In glsX function, check if $argv ends with `f`. Use `string match --quiet '.*f$' $argv`. If yes, set a flag variable `has_pretty=true` and remove trailing f from argv for the numeric part: `set -l base (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $argv)`. Then perform replacement: `string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $base`. Then if has_pretty, append `--pretty=full`. Use `commandline` maybe? Actually the abbr's function will be called to generate the command string, returning the string to be inserted. The function glsX currently simply outputs a string via `string replace`. So we can modify to something like:

```
function glsX
    set -l input $argv
    set -l has_pretty false
    if string match --quiet '*f' $input
        set has_pretty true
        set input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $input)
    end
    set -l command (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $input)
    if $has_pretty
        set command "$command --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $command
end
```

But need to preserve indentation and follow fish style: snake_case. Already function name is glsX (Camel?). Might want to keep same name.

Also need to adjust abbreviation regex: `abbr --regex 'gls\d+f?' --function glsX _glsX`. The existing abbreviation includes `_glsX` after `--function glsX` which is weird: they have `--function glsX _glsX`. Actually fish `abbr` command syntax: `abbr --add name expansion` but `abbr --regex` takes a regex and a function to produce expansion. According to fish docs: `abbr --regex <regex> --function <function>` I think they also have optional function arguments? Let's check typical usage: `abbr --regex 'gls[0-9]+' --function glsX`. The snippet has `abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX`. I suspect the extra `_glsX` is maybe the name of the abbr? Actually `abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX` will create an abbreviation that matches that regex and calls glsX with the matched string. The extra `_glsX` is likely the name of the abbreviation (the key?), but they might be using `abbr --add name expansion` but with regex they don't need name. I think fish `abbr` has syntax: `abbr --add <name> <expansion>` or `abbr --regex <regex> --function <function>`. There is no extra argument. So perhaps the code is wrong but works? It may treat `_glsX` as a second argument? Actually `abbr` can also accept a description? Not sure. Let's check fish doc: "abbr [OPTIONS] [NAME] [EXPANSION]" and `--regex` indicates that the NAME is a regular expression. `--function` indicates that the expansion is provided by a function (the function name). So you would have `abbr --regex 'gls[0-9]+' --function glsX`. There's no extra argument. The snippet `abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX` maybe uses `_glsX` as a description (like a hint). But not relevant.

We need to preserve indentation. The code snippet is:

```fish
abbr --regex 'gls\d+' --function glsX _glsX
function glsX
    string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $argv
end
```

We will modify to:

```
abbr --regex 'gls\d+f?' --function glsX _glsX
function glsX
    set -l input $argv
    # check for trailing f
    set -l has_pretty false
    if string match --quiet '*f' $input
        set has_pretty true
        set input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $input)
    end
    set -l cmd (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $input)
    if $has_pretty
        set cmd "$cmd --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $cmd
end
```

But we must keep fish style: use snake_case for variables. So maybe rename input to `raw_cmd` or `cmd_input`. Use `has_pretty`. Use `set -l` for local. Use `string replace` without `--`. We need to not add comments? The user says "Never add comments to the end of a line" but adding comments elsewhere is okay? The developer instruction says "Never add stupid comments" and "Prefer readable code over of comments." So we can add comments if they help readability, but not necessary. The user didn't explicitly forbid comments. But we can keep minimal comments, maybe none. Let's just implement without comments.

Thus final code snippet:

```
abbr --regex 'gls\d+f?' --function glsX _glsX
function glsX
    set -l cmd_input $argv
    set -l has_pretty false
    if string match --quiet '*f' $cmd_input
        set has_pretty true
        set cmd_input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $cmd_input)
    end
    set -l cmd (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $cmd_input)
    if $has_pretty
        set cmd "$cmd --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $cmd
end
```

Check fish syntax: `if string match --quiet '*f' $cmd_input` will match any string containing f anywhere, but we want trailing f. Use pattern `*f` matches trailing f? Actually `*f` matches any string ending with f (since fish uses glob). If we want regex, we could do `string match --quiet --regex 'f$' $cmd_input`. That might be clearer. So use `string match --quiet --regex 'f$' $cmd_input`.

Also note `if $has_pretty` may treat `$has_pretty` as a variable containing "true" or "false"? In fish, boolean condition is true if variable is non-empty and not zero. Setting `has_pretty` to `true` or empty works. We set `has_pretty` as empty initially, then set to `true` when needed. So `set -l has_pretty` initially empty (no value). Then `set has_pretty true`. Then `if $has_pretty` works. Simpler: use `set -l has_pretty` (no value) and later `set has_pretty`. Let's implement that.

Implementation:

```
function glsX
    set -l cmd_input $argv
    set -l has_pretty
    if string match --quiet --regex 'f$' $cmd_input
        set has_pretty
        set cmd_input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $cmd_input)
    end
    set -l cmd (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $cmd_input)
    if $has_pretty
        set cmd "$cmd --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $cmd
end
```

But setting `has_pretty` without a value makes it empty, which is false. We want true when set. So we can `set has_pretty true`. Then `if $has_pretty` works. Or use `set -l has_pretty false` and then `set has_pretty true`. Let's just set to true.

So final:

```
function glsX
    set -l cmd_input $argv
    set -l has_pretty false
    if string match --quiet --regex 'f$' $cmd_input
        set has_pretty true
        set cmd_input (string replace --regex 'f$' '' $cmd_input)
    end
    set -l cmd (string replace --regex '^gls' 'git log --stat -' $cmd_input)
    if $has_pretty
        set cmd "$cmd --pretty=full"
    end
    echo $cmd
end
```

Now ensure abbreviation regex matches digits and optional f: `'gls[0-9]+f?'` because fish regex uses `\d` maybe? In fish string match regex, `\d` is recognized? In fish regex, `\d` works as PCRE? The existing code uses `'gls\d+'`. So we can keep that and add optional f: `'gls\d+f?'`.

Thus modify abbr line to: `abbr --regex 'gls\d+f?' --function glsX _glsX`. Keep indentation same.

Potential issues: `$argv` will contain all arguments passed to function, but abbreviation calls the function with the matched string as a single argument. So `$argv` contains the matched string, no spaces. So the logic works.

Testing: typing `gls10` expands to `git log --stat -10`. typing `gls10f` expands to `git log --stat -10 --pretty=full`. Good.

Now also maybe they want to keep optional trailing f inserted like glsf? Actually glsf probably is defined somewhere else. Let's search. Not provided. But the request: "add optional trailing f which isnerts --pretty=full like glsf right above". So glsf is likely abbreviation for `git log --stat -` with full pretty. Let's search in the repository: there might be an abbreviation for glsf. But not needed.

Thus the code changes are as above.

We need to preserve indentation. The snippet originally had no extra blank lines. We must keep the original indentation of function body: one level (four spaces maybe). The snippet uses tabs? Actually fish code shows indent of 4 spaces. So we need to use same indentation.

Thus final answer: updated snippet with modifications.
